speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, Alex Stern, February 28th, 1996. Alex, could you tell me how long it took you to get to Gunthergrubbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very close to Auschwitz. I know we walked there. Exactly how long it took, I couldn't say. After you leave your father dead, and your brother is gone. You don't know where. And you see the situation in that area. The exact time from there to Gunthergrubbe-- I know we walked. We left Auschwitz, and we walked. We got to Gunthergrubbe. It was kind of a new camp. They had four barracks. It was like in middle of nowhere. There was a big field around. And they started building there new barracks. And that's the reason we went there-- to start building new barracks. And we started it with foundations. They had carpenters. They put-- they had roofers. They had all kinds of people working there. And we-- we know the name was Gunthergrubbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the way we found out it was near a coal mine. So we built more barracks. When we finished with the barracks, there were about, I would say, easy, 30 to 40 barracks. And they were preparing that place to get people to work in the mine. That was their main purpose. So we started working in the camp in the beginning. Everything has it's own barrack-- had its own barrack. There was a barrack with showers. There was a barrack that there were only toilets. There With a long, like a piece of wood. And they cut out holes in the middle, like 200 holes. And then they tell you, go into the barrack, you know when, and we went there. And we had to do whatever we had to do. That was only once a day. So then we went to work, and it looks like your body got trained-- whatever they want you to do. We worked all day. And at night, we went to sleep, and in the morning, [INAUDIBLE] not like in Auschwitz-- stay there for hours-- we used to go to work right away. And we carried all those bricks and whatever they needed to make the construction. So we were working in that camp, building about, easy, five, six months. And then the camp was enormous. And they started to bring in people. I remember the last people, who you wouldn't believe that there were still people in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought a big transport from Jews from Lodz. Before, we, you know, we didn't know what's going on on the outside. They were telling us, kind of a-- kind of a story-- because they all spoke Yiddish-- that they came from Lodz. There was the rest of the Lodz ghetto. When you talk about-- that was around about September already. So that was their last shipment from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. Oh! '44. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And from there, they started to put people working in the mine. So we used to leave in morning. It was dark when we left to go down the mine. Worked for 12 hours-- 12-hour shift. And then they had those little wagons pushed by people on rails. You shoveled coal for so long, and when the wagons were full, we pushed it. And then we started all over again. And I was working in the mine for about two months. And then they changed the shift. When those Lodz-- Jewish from Lodz came, they replaced us for a while. And they took the Lodz workers down. And then they took us again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to walk to the mine. It wasn't far. One thing I remember, when we walked from Gunthergrubbe to the mind, the mine was like a little town. It was-- we went into a big building. And that was under the-- going underneath the ground. It was [? rough there, ?] I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a camp between. And one morning, there was nobody in the camp. And there were SS men all over the place. We didn't know what's going. And they made us run so we wouldn't see anything happen there. It was something big, big happened there. When we got to the camp, we find out there was a Russian camp there, a concentration camp. And somehow-- if it was with help from outside-- but everything disappeared. But when we got there, already it must have been cleaned up. And they just made us run through that area. So that's one thing I remember from the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think happened to the Russians who were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They disappeared. Nobody knew, nobody. Maybe they took them to another camp. The-- the rumors were that somehow, they got out of there. And then there was another occasion that I wanted to mention-- is this one, one when we came home to the camp. You see, the camp was built with wires around, all around the place. It was enormous, tall. And-- but you could see through. One day we came home, we saw those gray blankets that they were using in the camp. And all the-- the gates, the wire gates, were covered. We couldn't see inside, nothing. Nobody knew what's happening. See, we were away for 12 hours. We used to go away for 12 hours, come back, and go again for 12 hours. So when we went outside, there was a big, big, big rush there. There were a lot of people standing in line there. And we were maybe the last group to get out of the mine or from wherever they brought them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stood in line for about two hours, till everybody got lined up. SS men with machine guns-- and nobody knew what's happening. So we were standing and standing. And suddenly, a black truck came. The truck came in. It stopped. Five people came out of the truck. And there were a gallow prepared for five people. So they made them go up to the gallows. There were five-- how do you call it-- tied up to the gallow, like those where they hang people on those-- what do you call those-- [INAUDIBLE] or whatever. And they send those five people up there. And then the German, the commandant-- and talking about, I'll explain you about him later. He was a very good-looking guy. He spoke I don't know how many languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: He stood up in front of them, and he started  to say, first in German, that these five people were trying to escape. And they were caught. And they were brought back. But they were well-fed. They worked in the kitchen. We were already, you know, losing our skin. And they tried to escape, and they're going to be punished for that. And then he said it, I think, in Russian. Even Hungarian he spoke, or maybe only those few words. He explained to everybody that they're going to be hung because they were trying to escape. So finally, when they-- it was already almost dark, they gave the order to pull the strings, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were four people a little bit older. The fifth one was a young guy, was, I say, mostly 17, 18 years old. And when they pulled the string on him, he said, Shema Yisreal. And everybody was like-- you couldn't even breathe. It  was the first time, really, we saw an official hanging, because there was dead people we saw every night. We got up in the morning, the next guy to you didn't wake up. But just officially hanging live people, that was the first time we really saw one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was terrified out of themselves. So I guess that was the rules. And this is things you remember. I mean, you memorialize. Because you can't forget. But people died, they died. And they died, and maybe they went to a better world, whatever we called it in those years. But this was the most terrifying thing I've ever seen in my life-- until then, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was until we were in Gunthergrubbe. And we were there till beginning of-- end of December, beginning of January. What was the food situation there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was better than in Auschwitz. We had breakfast-- I mean for breakfast. There was a-- we  called it a brick bread, because it was made out of god knows what. It didn't taste like bread at all, but if you eat it for few months, you got used to it. And they used to take a bread, like a loaf of bread, and they used to cut it in 10 pieces. And it was for every 10 people-- they gave you one bread. So they gave you the bread. A lot of people figured if it's a piece of bread-- it was smaller than a slice-- slice of bread they slice here in the bakery shop. A lot of people took that piece, and finished it off, and they figured, I eat once. And a lot of people were dividing it in three parts, in four parts. And, eh, it was part of life in those years. But it was some-- something to do through the day. And then at night, when we came home, there was one bowl of soup, they used to call it. There was everything in it, you know, like-- like god knows, leaves from what, bitter leaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave you a bowl of soup. And in those years, it tasted like food, so we-- we thought we have food. So it-- it kept us alive. That's all I can say about it. But we never-- we could never eat full. The only thing I do remember, whoever had a little bit of protection-- the food came in those big containers. And, you know, when you take the food out with a big spoon, or whatever you call it, there was always something left in it. So they had a chance to lick the container out. And they used to kill each other to get to the container.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being I was a small guy, I never had a chance to get close to one of those containers. And I didn't mind, because by the time people finished there, they were bruised, and all over their bodies. It was a fight. It was a-- food was our main concern. But that was our problem. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take care of you when you were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be very honest with you, I was very, very, very lucky. Whoever got sick, I don't think never survived. If you got sick, there was nobody to cure it. And if you're sick, and you don't eat, and you don't take medicine, and you don't rest, you just fall apart. And that's it. And every-- every morning they picked up the dead people. That-- that was routine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would say, I was so lucky that I look backwards-- really sick. I was never really sick. I mean, weak, hungry-- you name it-- heartbroken, desperate, everything else. But I wasn't sick. That's one reason I survived, I guess. Were you able to keep up with the Jewish calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all. The last day when we were in Auschwitz with my father, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. And my father was the only man in that group that knew by heart the whole services of [NON-ENGLISH]. Because he was at home, he used to read the Torah, he used to do everything. So he was there who-- and the last, I would say, religious services that I ever, ever in the war, had was that one. After that, we didn't know what was-- we didn't even know the date so well. We only knew-- this one, you really wouldn't believe. Every Saturday night in Gunthergrubbe, this camp commandant had a show-- a show. Every Sunday night we had actors, singers. We had-- they used to play a fiddle, whatever they had. The show lasted for two hours. And you mind was-- tomorrow, you were going to survive. But there was a show every Sunday night in Gunthergrubbe. And he-- he did it, that commandant of the camp. That was their system. [INAUDIBLE] Show the world that we have fun here, or whatever they wanted to show with that. But it was a show.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the entertainers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were three brothers. I'll take about them later. But they were real actors. The could sing. They could play. The could dance. And they got a little more food than our regular people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. They were Jewish prisoners from any part of the world, wherever they came from. Those were only three. But there were others. Singing. We used to sing Yiddish songs and whatever. That's what they did in the camp Sunday night for two hours. Then it was hell after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to enjoy this concert?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, you may have enjoyed it, but your mind was somewhere else. That's for sure. It was two hours that you listened to it. But you just worried if I survive tomorrow. And you were still hungry, even-- even when the concert went on. That's the way it was. German-- German system-- give you music, but kill you outside. That's the way it was in Gunthergrubbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see the Red Cross, or receive packages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Never heard of it-- not to see, you know. In the camp? Never knew it existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out that you were going to leave the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when it came end of December and beginning of January, we heard a lot of shooting and bombing and god knows what. And we felt the war is getting close. And I saw the Germans very, very nervous. And-- and one night, they picked us out. I would say, well, I can't go by numbers because of that camp was so big. They were building a big factory near to us. And they were talking about from coal, making 27 different items. That was the-- even they said, they're going to make margarine out of coal. In those years, you-- you heard something like this. But it was enormous. It was about 15 stories high. They were building it unbelievably near our camp. And it was in the middle of building it when this started to-- come that the war nearer, and nearer, and nearer. And then one night they picked us all up, and they made us walk out of the camp. And one particular thing-- I remember that there was-- the SS men took their belongings. And they put it on a wagon. And they took about 10 of us to push the wagon. And that saved our lives. Because if you hold on to a wagon, you push, you push, and you go with the wagon. You go by yourself-- it was snow and cold. You fall down and you're dead. That's one thing I remember. The other thing I remembered, it was a box, a cardboard box. So, you know, in the dark, I started to make a hole in the box, see what I can find in it. There was shoe polish. And finally, I came up with something you wouldn't believe. It was toothpaste. I don't know what kind in those years. But I broke it, and it was sweet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [INAUDIBLE]. What did they make toothpaste out of? I was chewing it and chewing it. And finally, you know, there was no more. It was a little bit sweet. It helped. Everything helped. And I think that little wagon saved our life. We were about eight people pushing it. How long ago did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god. Ah. I-- I could not give you an exact date. But I know we started in our camp. And we went to a big city. They stopped everything on the way in the city. They-- they cordoned off everything. And we went in the street. And all the time, go, go, go, go-- shooting and hollering and dogs and god knows what. And until we paid-- It was a big city. I think it was Gliwice, because I followed it, more or less, after the war. At that time, I didn't know what city it was. Gliwice or Katowice, one of the two. It was big, because we walked there for about hours till we came to the other end of the city. Then we slowed down. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying that the Nazis didn't let the people come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only they wouldn't let the people out of the house, I don't think there were no people in those houses. They were trying to isolate one street there and get-- I mean, that was only-- when we got there already, there hundreds of camps from the area-- thousands and thousands of people, just like-- like cattle. When you drive cattle through the town, that's exactly the way, today, I look back at it. Pushing one another--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't. Nothing. And we went-- at night we went through that city. Whatever it was, no-- no sound of everything. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Snow. It was cold, very cold. There was snow. There was ice on the floor. But I remember we had those Dutch wooden shoes, whatever you call it. We filled it out with paper. We tied it to our foot. And that was good, because those shoes kept some warm in it. And we put straw in it. We put cement paper-- we worked by the building. We filled it out with whatever. We put paper around our-- paper keeps your feet-- your body warm. Everywhere. Even around my back. And we put paper all over our bodies to keep it-- keep it warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get food and water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No food and water. We got something when we left the camp. And then I-- we didn't stop till we passed that city. They did give us something for the, you know, for the way. We didn't know where we're going. We didn't know what's doing. We didn't get food till we get out of that city. Then we stopped. And there was like, like we ate. At least we had snow, you know. People were having liquid. So we used to chew on the snow whenever we could. And like giving food-- after that, a few days, we stopped by a-- a big barn. I remember there was a barn there. And they-- they stopped there. And we did get something. I really don't know what because, at that time already we were frozen and cold and tired, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the ones who couldn't keep up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we knew you heard shooting all over. So most of the time, I was in the middle of the group, because we were pushing this wagon. This wagon belonged to the SS. So, you know, they wanted us to hold onto that wagon. What happened in the rear end, I did not see. We heard shooting. And there was the rumor, whoever falls back, is never going to  make it. So we went more by what we heard than what we saw at that time. Today, you know, you read, and you find out. But at that time, that was my knowledge and feeling what was happening there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did this take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly-- I know names. I don't know names. But we-- we went, we took a train. They put us, again, on here, in cattle wagons, in open wagons. We went by a train until-- once there was a-- an allied bombardment somewhere around there. Then they made us all get out of the train. And a lot of people got hit there, because, you know, the Americans, they were bombing near the train station. They didn't know who we are. The SS ran out of the wagons. And a lot of people were trying to escape, but I don't believe-- nobody made it. That was already in Germany. The only thing I know-- that from there we went to Oranienburg. That was Sachsenhausen. That was another enormous, enormous camp. God knows what was in there. There's so many people there already-- French, Italian. I remember once, a unit of Italians-- I remember their hat. There was those pointy hats. And they were talking Italian. And being I understood Romanian, I could, you know, a little bit find about them. So they just said that they were taking prisoners because Italy didn't follow German orders. They were trying to tell us something. But there were so many prisoners in Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions there? Did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did get something to eat there, yes. You see, we were in a-- you know they call the hangers, those airplane hangers? And if I say, not thousands, tens of thousands of people-- god knows from where they came. Such a-- such a mix up. Such a panic. And people were dropping out, and falling out. It was-- it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country were you in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Germany. Oranienburg is very, very close to Berlin-- Sachsenhausen. There we were in Germany. How long we were there? Maybe a month. Then we were again walking. And we went by train, and walking again. And they never gave-- gave us up. They kept us pushing, and taking us, and always added people, too, from other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we could see groups of people coming from all over. And we walked. We walked. We walked until half of us disappeared. And then in our last stop was Regensburg. So Regensburg was, I guess, as far as they could take us. But we went between in so many places that I really had no knowledge. I mean in the war, who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it looked like years, whatever went by there. So finally, we wound up in Regensburg. We were there for about, I would say, maybe-- maybe two or three weeks. And one day, they started pulling out of there. We saw the German soldiers changing uniform. They took them off, and put on civilian clothes. And something that we figured out something happening. Because and then-- but they never let us go. They always kept, you know, SS with us with machine guns. And-- and when we walked out of-- of Flossenburg, we went on a highway. And we could hear shooting and shooting and shooting and bombing. And it was-- it was terrible. It went so far that-- that the ears were numb from those big noises. We couldn't-- we couldn't even talk to each other. And besides that, we never knew nobody who walks next to us. If-- if whoever left with us from our camp, or disappeared, or god knows where he was. New people from all over-- prisoners, soldiers, gypsies, you name it. There were all kind of people with us. Anyway, once they stopped, and they said to us, now you want to-- you want to save your life. Run down the tree by the highway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went out of Regensburg. It was-- the exact town, I couldn't mention. But after Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
